State of California

Memorandum

To    : David Pitman, Chief
        Political Reform Division
        111 Capitol Mall

Date  : April 5, 1978

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Technical Assistance and Analysis Division

Subject:

This memorandum is in response to your January 27, 1978, request
for guidance regarding the appropriateness of lobbyist employers
filing consolidated periodic reports.

Government Code Section 86108(a) states in relevant part:

     "... the following persons shall file ... statements ...
     (a) any person who employs or contracts for the
     services of one or more lobbyists, whether independently
     or jointly with other persons ..."

It is the staff's view that, in general, a parent corporation and
its wholly-owned subsidiaries should be permitted to file consoli-
dated periodic reports in every case. In all other cases, however,
consolidated reporting should not be permitted without our specific
approval.

Of the examples which you provided with your January 27, 1978
memorandum, and as supplemented by your March 17, 1978 memorandum,
the lobbyist employers which file consolidated periodic reports
are for the most part parents and their wholly-owned subsidiaries.
There were two exceptions. "Continental Heller Corporation and
Swinerton and Walberg Company, a joint venture" and "Southern
California Rock Products Association and Southern California
Ready Mixed Concrete Association" are also listed as filing consol-
idated periodic reports but they are not parents and subsidiaries.

It is the staff's opinion that the joint venture between "Continental
Heller Corporation and Swinerton and Walberg Company" should be
permitted to file a consolidated report since a single entity, the
joint venture, is employing the lobbyist.

Regarding the example of the "Southern California Rock Products
Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association,"
we are not presented with any specifics of their relationship and
cannot reach a determination as to the appropriateness of a consoli-
dated filing in their case. Therefore, we conclude they should file
separate reports until such time as additional facts are provided
and a determination can be made.

David, Pitman                           - 2 -                    April 5, 1978


Finally, we recommend that your office establish a system of cross-
referencing, if one does not exist, for those cases where consolidated
reports are filed by other than a parent and its wholly-owned sub-
sidiaries.

If I can be of further assistance, do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,


Alan Herndon
Compliance Representative

State of California

Memorandum

To      :   Alan Herndon
           Fair Political Practices Commission

Date:  January 27, 1978

From  :   Secretary of State - DAVID B. PITMAN, Manager
          Political Reform Division

Subject:  Consolidated Reports Filed by Lobbyist Employers


In April, 1977, we were given qualified approval by the Commission staff to
allow consolidated statements to be filed by Chevron USA, Inc. and Standard
Oil Company of California based on the determination that they were "acting
in concert."

We have the situation where Southern California Rock Products Association
and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association have been filing
consolidated statements.  I understand that you have advised this office
that in this instance such consolidation should not be allowed.

At the present time, Southern California Rock Products Association and
Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association file separate reports.
Separate reporting began with the September, 1977, report at the suggestion
of R. Slagle, an auditor with the Franchise Tax Board.

In view of Government Code Sections 86108(a) and 82047, we have allowed
lobbyist employers to file consolidated reports.  Would you specifically
advise with respect to reports submitted by Southern California Rock
Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Asso-
ciation why consolidation is inappropriate and provide guidelines to this
agency on when such consolidations should be allowed.

State of California

PART 5--PAYMENTS TO INFLUENCE LEGISLATIVE OR ADMINISTRATIVE ACTION (other than to a Lobbyist)

"Payments to influence legislative or administrative action" (Gov. Code Section 82045) include but are not limited to payment of expenses incurred by you on behalf of or in support of the lobbyist or his activities, gifts, including gifts of food and beverages to elective state officials, legislative officials, agency officials and their immediate families and include also any payments made by you in connection with direct communications with any such persons or in connection with urging others to enter into direct communications with such persons. Compensation to your employees and the cost of operating your facilities utilized in such communications may have to be reported. (See Instruction Manual and FPPC Regulations) Payments of $25 or more (other than to your own employees) must be itemized.

Attachment - Period 12/1/77 through 12/31/77 
Explanation With Respect To Items Identified With An Asterisk (*). 
1. 
It is assumed that to influence or attempt to influence 
administrative action requires a "communication" with regard to a 
"proceeding." 
Thus, only "proceeding" numbers which have been the 
subject of communications (including agreeing to hearing dates, 
filing pleadings, etc.) are listed. 
None of the proceedings are 
rate making proceedings in the usual sense of the term. 
However, 
the question of whether other proceedings before the Public 
Utilities Commission are "quasi-legislative" or "quasi-judicial" is 
often a difficult question which has only been partially clarified 
by regulations issued to date and which may be subject to dispute in 
many instances. 
To be conservative, in all questionable cases the 
proceeding has arbitrarily been treated as being quasi-legislative 
and some or all of the proceedings listed may actually be entirely 
judicial in nature. 
For this reason, much or all of the amounts shown 
for payments received and payments made may actually be erroneously 
reported because not in any way connected with attempts to influence 
"administrative action". 
2. 
In Column (a) on page 1, silari has been allocated by 
computing the number of days covered by the report, excluding 
Saturdays and Sundays (but not excluding holidays, vacation days or 
days not worked because of illness) and, assuming that each day is 
a 7-1/2 bour day, dividing the total into the hours expended in 
"connection", directly or indirectly, with what might possibly be 
considered to be lobbying activities to Qbtain a percentage which, 
in turn, was applied to the salary received for the period covered 
by the report. 
This appears to be the only reasonably practical 
method and almost certainly allocates a larger portion of salary to 
"lobbying" than is actually the case. 
3. 
All amounts shown in Column (c) on page 1 are either advances 
or airline tickets (a personal maintenance expense) charged directly 
to the employers account. 
In addition to salary and advance(s) shown 
under Columns (a) and (c), on page 1, other various fringe benefits 
were received, such as medical insurance, etc., the value of which 
is impossible and impractical to calculate or obtain for purposes 
of the lobbyist report and therefore have not been included. 
Also 
since only a portion of my time as an attorney-at-law is spent in 
activities related to the handling of proceedings before the 
California Public Utilities Commission, the amount shown as an advance 
is an allocated portion of total advances based upon ~stimate of .", 
~mount reasonably necessary to cover personal expense~ to be incurred. 
" 
~, 
: 
. 
" 
. 
4. 
To the extent possible, all disbursement items are posted 
and reported according to date incurred rather than date paid. 
Personal maintenance expenses, including reimbursement for use of 
personal automobiles, are reported in amounts reimbursed by employer 
since no other possible or practical method is available and any 
attempt to use any other basis would result in providing less 
accurate information.

sections.question_synthetic: The Political Reform Division seeks guidance on whether lobbyist employers filing consolidated periodic reports should be permitted to do so, specifically addressing whether the Southern California Rock Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association can file consolidated reports and what general guidelines should apply.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that parent corporations and wholly-owned subsidiaries may file consolidated periodic reports, but all other consolidated reporting requires specific FPPC approval. Joint ventures may be permitted to file as single entities, while the specific associations mentioned should file separate reports pending further determination.

Additional forms and attachments are included but are less relevant to the core advice question.

Attachment - Period 12/1/77 through 12/31/77 
Explanation With Respect To Items Identified With An Asterisk (*). 
1. 
It is assumed that to influence or attempt to influence 
administrative action requires a "communication" with regard to a 
"proceeding." 
Thus, only "proceeding" numbers which have been the 
subject of communications (including agreeing to hearing dates, 
filing pleadings, etc.) are listed. 
None of the proceedings are 
rate making proceedings in the usual sense of the term. 
However, 
the question of whether other proceedings before the Public 
Utilities Commission are "quasi-legislative" or "quasi-judicial" is 
often a difficult question which has only been partially clarified 
by regulations issued to date and which may be subject to dispute in 
many instances. 
To be conservative, in all questionable cases the 
proceeding has arbitrarily been treated as being quasi-legislative 
and some or all of the proceedings listed may actually be entirely 
judicial in nature. 
For this reason, much or all of the amounts shown 
for payments received and payments made may actually be erroneously 
reported because not in any way connected with attempts to influence 
"administrative action". 
2. 
In Column (a) on page 1, silari has been allocated by 
computing the number of days covered by the report, excluding 
Saturdays and Sundays (but not excluding holidays, vacation days or 
days not worked because of illness) and, assuming that each day is 
a 7-1/2 bour day, dividing the total into the hours expended in 
"connection", directly or indirectly, with what might possibly be 
considered to be lobbying activities to Qbtain a percentage which, 
in turn, was applied to the salary received for the period covered 
by the report. 
This appears to be the only reasonably practical 
method and almost certainly allocates a larger portion of salary to 
"lobbying" than is actually the case. 
3. 
All amounts shown in Column (c) on page 1 are either advances 
or airline tickets (a personal maintenance expense) charged directly 
to the employers account. 
In addition to salary and advance(s) shown 
under Columns (a) and (c), on page 1, other various fringe benefits 
were received, such as medical insurance, etc., the value of which 
is impossible and impractical to calculate or obtain for purposes 
of the lobbyist report and therefore have not been included. 
Also 
since only a portion of my time as an attorney-at-law is spent in 
activities related to the handling of proceedings before the 
California Public Utilities Commission, the amount shown as an advance 
is an allocated portion of total advances based upon ~stimate of .",

embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To : David Pitman, Chief Political Reform Division 111 Capitol Mall Date : April 5, 1978 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Technical Assistance and Analysis Division Subject: This memorandum is in response to your January 27, 1978, request for guidance regarding the appropriateness of lobbyist employers filing consolidated periodic reports. Government Code Section 86108(a) states in relevant part: "... the following persons shall file ... statements ... (a) any person who employs or contracts for the services of one or more lobbyists, whether independently or jointly with other persons ..." It is the staff's view that, in general, a parent corporation and its wholly-owned subsidiaries should be permitted to file consoli- dated periodic reports in every case. In all other cases, however, consolidated reporting should not be permitted without our specific approval. Of the examples which you provided with your January 27, 1978 memorandum, and as supplemented by your March 17, 1978 memorandum, the lobbyist employers which file consolidated periodic reports are for the most part parents and their wholly-owned subsidiaries. There were two exceptions. "Continental Heller Corporation and Swinerton and Walberg Company, a joint venture" and "Southern California Rock Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association" are also listed as filing consol- idated periodic reports but they are not parents and subsidiaries. It is the staff's opinion that the joint venture between "Continental Heller Corporation and Swinerton and Walberg Company" should be permitted to file a consolidated report since a single entity, the joint venture, is employing the lobbyist. Regarding the example of the "Southern California Rock Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association," we are not presented with any specifics of their relationship and cannot reach a determination as to the appropriateness of a consoli- dated filing in their case. Therefore, we conclude they should file separate reports until such time as additional facts are provided and a determination can be made. David, Pitman - 2 - April 5, 1978 Finally, we recommend that your office establish a system of cross- referencing, if one does not exist, for those cases where consolidated reports are filed by other than a parent and its wholly-owned sub- sidiaries. If I can be of further assistance, do not hesitate to contact me. Sincerely, Alan Herndon Compliance Representative AH:bw State of California Memorandum To : Alan Herndon Fair Political Practices Commission Date: January 27, 1978 From : Secretary of State - DAVID B. PITMAN, Manager Political Reform Division Subject: Consolidated Reports Filed by Lobbyist Employers In April, 1977, we were given qualified approval by the Commission staff to allow consolidated statements to be filed by Chevron USA, Inc. and Standard Oil Company of California based on the determination that they were "acting in concert." We have the situation where Southern California Rock Products Association and Southern California Ready Mixed Concrete Association have been filing consolidated statements. I understand that you have advised this office that in this instance such consolidation should not be allowed.
embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to a January 27, 1978 request regarding the appropriateness of consolidated periodic reporting by lobbyist employers under Government Code Section 86108(a). The FPPC provides guidelines distinguishing when consolidated reporting is permissible (parent-subsidiary relationships) versus when it requires specific approval (other entities).
